article_title: Astronomers Discover a Planetary System Whose Orbits Are Visibly Changing in Real Time, a Phenomenon Never Before Observed
summary: Round 2 review: new misattribution introduced in the rewrite — quote credited to Triaud is actually Guillot's in the Birmingham source.

findings.message: Source fetched via Archive.org fallback (original returned 200)
Quote misattributed to Triaud — belongs to Tristan Guillot
findings.details: The passage reads: 'providing, in Triaud's words, "a glimpse of what happens shortly after planet formation"'. The University of Birmingham source attributes this exact quote to Tristan Guillot of Observatoire de la Côte d'Azur, not Amaury Triaud. The rewrite agent correctly replaced the spurious earlier quote but credited it to the wrong scientist. Fix: either attribute to Guillot (and update the surrounding sentence accordingly), or find and use a verbatim Triaud quote from the same source.

content_preview.title: Astronomers Discover a Planetary System Whose Orbits Are Visibly Changing in Real Time, a Phenomenon Never Before Observed
content_preview.summary: The TOI-201 system hosts a super-Earth, a warm Jupiter, and a record-setting brown dwarf whose gravitational interplay drives orbital shifts observable on human timescales — within 200 years its planets will stop transiting.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

Astronomers have uncovered a planetary system unlike any other in the known catalog: a three-body system 370 light-years from Earth whose orbits are shifting fast enough to track across a human lifetime. The discovery, published April 15 in *Science Advances*, is rare enough that researchers say it offers a live window into gravitational dynamics that typically play out over millions of years.

The system, designated TOI-201, was identified using a combination of NASA's TESS space t...

recommendations: Correct the misattributed Triaud quote: the 'glimpse of what happens shortly after planet formation' is Tristan Guillot's, per the Birmingham source. Either credit Guillot or use a verified Triaud quote.

editor_notes.content_quality: Rounds 1 findings on unsourced record claim, unsourced star age comparison, and spurious Triaud quote have all been addressed. The article is well-structured and covers the TOI-201 system comprehensively. However, a new attribution error was introduced in the rewrite.
editor_notes.source_verification: Space.com (source 1): fetched and verified — 370 light-years ✓, three-planet system with correct masses/periods ✓, TESS/ASTEP/LCOGT instruments ✓, 200-year transit cessation ✓, Science Advances April 15 ✓. University of Birmingham (source 2): fetched and verified — star 30% more massive and 30% larger than Sun ✓, about a tenth of Sun's age ✓, Gaia satellite contribution ✓, Concordia Station ✓, Triaud affiliation (University of Birmingham) ✓. HOWEVER: the quote 'a glimpse of what happens shortly after planet formation' is attributed to Tristan Guillot (Observatoire de la Côte d'Azur) in the Birmingham source, NOT Triaud. The article incorrectly attributes it 'in Triaud's words'. Phys.org (source 3): fetched via Archive.org — longest-period transiting object claim ✓, Mireles quote verbatim ✓, Dragomir quote verbatim ✓, Mireles as UNM PhD candidate ✓, Dragomir as UNM Professor ✓.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: All Round 1 corrections are accurate. The new error is the Triaud/Guillot attribution. All other facts confirmed from sources.
concerns: Quote 'a glimpse of what happens shortly after planet formation' is attributed to Triaud in the article but belongs to Tristan Guillot per the Birmingham source.
recommendations: Correct the attribution from Triaud to Guillot for the 'glimpse of what happens shortly after planet formation' quote, or replace with a verified Triaud quote from the Birmingham source.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: The three Round 1 issues are resolved, but the rewrite introduced a quote misattribution. One targeted fix needed before publication.
